From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Pakistani television shows and dramas

**Pakistani dramas**, or **Pakistani serials**, are [televised serials](/wiki/Serial_(radio_and_television) "Serial (radio and television)") produced in [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"). Although most of the serials are produced in [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu"), an increasing number are produced in other [Pakistani languages](/wiki/Pakistani_languages "Pakistani languages") such as [Sindhi](/wiki/Sindhi_language "Sindhi language"), [Pashto](/wiki/Pashto_language "Pashto language"), [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_language "Punjabi language") and [Balochi](/wiki/Balochi_language "Balochi language"). One of Pakistan's oldest television dramas is the Urdu serial *[Khuda Ki Basti](/wiki/Khuda_Ki_Basti_(TV_series) "Khuda Ki Basti (TV series)")*, which aired in 1969.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Pakistani dramas, like serials elsewhere, reflect the country's culture. According to critics, the decades of the 1970s and 1980s are considered to be the golden age of Pakistani serials.[[2]](#cite_note-:6-2)

In 2016, a *[Dawn News](/wiki/Dawn_News "Dawn News")* report estimated that the five major entertainment channels combined produce around 80 dramas every year, with an average of 16 dramas per channel.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

They have helped to attract viewers nationwide to television.[[4]](#cite_note-auto-4) The serials are widely popular in [South Asian](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia") countries, including [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan"),[[5]](#cite_note-geo.tv-5) [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh"),[[6]](#cite_note-tribune.com.pk-6) [India](/wiki/India "India") and [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Origin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pakistani_dramas&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Origin")]

Many Pakistani dramas are based on [Urdu novels](/wiki/Pakistani_literature "Pakistani literature"). In the years following 1969, many authors, such as [Umera Ahmad](/wiki/Umera_Ahmad "Umera Ahmad") and [Farhat Ishtiaq](/wiki/Farhat_Ishtiaq "Farhat Ishtiaq"), became television writers. Both Umera and Farhat have written content for [digests](/wiki/Pakistani_literature#Digests "Pakistani literature") as well as television serials.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) In recent years, Pakistani dramas have increasingly begun to tackle social issues that are considered taboos.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## Social impact

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pakistani_dramas&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Social impact")]

[Pakistani feminists](/wiki/Feminism_in_Pakistan "Feminism in Pakistan") are usually concerned about the depiction of women in Pakistani drama TV serials, they receive many of those with skepticism & reservation.[[11]](#cite_note-:1-11)[[12]](#cite_note-:0-12) UK based Pakistani feminist Tasneem Ahmar, whose research institute focuses on the women-media relationships, complaints 99.99% of TV drama in Pakistan is misogynistic, patriarchal, medieval in its depiction and treatment of [women's](/wiki/Women_in_Pakistan "Women in Pakistan") issues.[[13]](#cite_note-:52-13) Ahmar says there is no doubt that Pakistani Drama TV serials are hugely popular among all strata of Pakistani society, but unfortunately they waste their potential of doing better in projecting progressive values rather than regressive values of women's depiction & support to equal rights.[[13]](#cite_note-:52-13)

## Popularity outside Pakistan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pakistani_dramas&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Popularity outside Pakistan")]

See also: [List of Pakistani television series](/wiki/List_of_Pakistani_television_series "List of Pakistani television series")

### Middle East

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pakistani_dramas&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Middle East")]

In 2013, the Pakistani drama *[Humsafar](/wiki/Humsafar "Humsafar")* was dubbed into [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") and broadcast by [MBC](/wiki/Middle_East_Broadcasting_Center "Middle East Broadcasting Center") in the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East") as *Rafeeq-Al-Rooh*.[[14]](#cite_note-14) The show was immediately successful and, after its first few episodes were broadcast, became the channel's most-watched drama. Before this, Pakistani dramas were not broadcast in the Middle East. After *Humasafar*, other shows such as *[Malaal](/wiki/Malaal "Malaal")* (aired as *Hob-Wa-Nadam*), *[Zindagi Gulzar Hai](/wiki/Zindagi_Gulzar_Hai "Zindagi Gulzar Hai")* (aired as *Asrar Al Hob*), *[Khaani](/wiki/Khaani "Khaani")*, *[Aisi Hai Tanhai](/wiki/Aisi_Hai_Tanhai "Aisi Hai Tanhai")*, *[Suno Chanda](/wiki/Suno_Chanda "Suno Chanda")* and several others were also dubbed into Arabic and broadcast by MBC.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pakistani_dramas&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: India")]

Dramas such as *Deewarein*, *[Waris](/wiki/Waris_(serial) "Waris (serial)")*, and *[Jungle](/wiki/Jungle_(PTV_series) "Jungle (PTV series)")* were popular in India during the 1980s:[[18]](#cite_note-toi-18) according to a 1988 *[India Today](/wiki/India_Today "India Today")* report which described it as an "invasion", Pakistani serials such as *[Tanhaiyan](/wiki/Tanhaiyaan "Tanhaiyaan")*, *[Ankahi](/wiki/Ankahi "Ankahi")* and *[Sona Chandi](/wiki/Sona_Chandi "Sona Chandi")* gained remarkable popularity in India, to the extent that demand for their video cassettes rivaled or even surpassed that of Hindi films in some regions. According to the report, video libraries across [North India](/wiki/North_India "North India") and [Western India](/wiki/Western_India "Western India"), with cities like Delhi, Mumbai, Lucknow, Jodhpur, Srinagar, and throughout Punjab widely stocked these Pakistani serials, with customers often returning repeatedly for the same titles. In Bombay, where all of the 3,000 video librairies stocked cassettes of Pakistani dramas as per the *India Today* report, their popularity was described as comparable to that of a major Bollywood release, such as [Amitabh Bachchan](/wiki/Amitabh_Bachchan "Amitabh Bachchan") films, offering Indian audiences a sense of nostalgia and a level of sophistication that critics argued was missing from contemporary Indian television. The phenomenon was partly attributed to the polished direction, strong acting, "technical perfection" and the emotional resonance these serials carried, evoking memories of pre-Partition cultural connections.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Eventually, the [Indian government](/wiki/Government_of_India "Government of India") imposed a ban on Pakistani television channels in India.[[18]](#cite_note-toi-18) In 2009, the [Senate of Pakistan](/wiki/Senate_of_Pakistan "Senate of Pakistan")'s broadcasting division appealed to the [Parliament of India](/wiki/Parliament_of_India "Parliament of India") to lift the ban. In 2012, India began debating whether to reverse the ban on Pakistani television channels. India assured Pakistan that it would consider a proposal by Pakistani foreign secretary Jalil Abbas Jilani to lift the ban.[[18]](#cite_note-toi-18)

Zee Entertainment Enterprises (ZEEL) launched an entertainment television channel, [Zindagi](/wiki/Zindagi_(TV_channel) "Zindagi (TV channel)"), on 23 June 2014.[[20]](#cite_note-ab-20) The channel aired syndicated television shows from Pakistan, and has been well received.[[21]](#cite_note-Hindustan_Times-21)[[22]](#cite_note-cb-22)[[23]](#cite_note-z-23) It has been criticized, however, for showing dramas with a smaller number of episodes. To appease viewers,[[24]](#cite_note-tvc-24)[[25]](#cite_note-nn-25)[[26]](#cite_note-gg-26) the channel aired hundreds of Pakistani television shows since then including *[Aunn Zara](/wiki/Aunn_Zara "Aunn Zara")*, *[Humsafar](/wiki/Humsafar "Humsafar")*, *[Pyarey Afzal](/wiki/Pyarey_Afzal "Pyarey Afzal")*, *[Zindagi Gulzar Hai](/wiki/Zindagi_Gulzar_Hai "Zindagi Gulzar Hai")* and *[Meray Paas Tum Ho](/wiki/Meray_Paas_Tum_Ho "Meray Paas Tum Ho")*.[[20]](#cite_note-ab-20)[[22]](#cite_note-cb-22)[[23]](#cite_note-z-23)[[25]](#cite_note-nn-25)[[26]](#cite_note-gg-26)[[27]](#cite_note-bb-27) *Zindagi Gulzar Hai* became so popular that it was re-run one month after it ended its initial run in India.[[27]](#cite_note-bb-27) In September 2016, however, Zindagi dropped all Pakistani shows from their line-up. In this way, [Zindagi](/wiki/Zindagi_(TV_channel) "Zindagi (TV channel)") came to its end on television.[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)

In July 2020, it was announced that [Zindagi](/wiki/Zindagi_(TV_channel) "Zindagi (TV channel)") will make its comeback digitally and brand will come with its 5 original Web Series including *[Mrs. & Mr. Shameem](/wiki/Mrs._%26_Mr._Shameem "Mrs. & Mr. Shameem")*, *Abdullah Pur Ka Devdas*, *[Churails](/wiki/Churails "Churails")*, *[Ek Jhoothi Love Story](/w/index.php?title=Ek_Jhoothi_Love_Story&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ek Jhoothi Love Story (page does not exist)")* and *[Dhoop Ki Deewar](/wiki/Dhoop_Ki_Deewar "Dhoop Ki Deewar")* along with some old and new shows from Zindagi Library.

Some already broadcast television series and some of the recent years such as *[Diyar-e-Dil](/wiki/Diyar-e-Dil "Diyar-e-Dil")*, *[Mera Naam Yousuf Hai](/wiki/Mera_Naam_Yousuf_Hai "Mera Naam Yousuf Hai")*, *[Mann Mayal](/wiki/Mann_Mayal "Mann Mayal")*, *[Baaghi](/wiki/Baaghi_(TV_series) "Baaghi (TV series)")*, *[Behadd](/wiki/Behadd "Behadd")*, *[O Rangreza](/wiki/O_Rangreza "O Rangreza")*, *[Ullu Baraye Farokht Nahi](/wiki/Ullu_Baraye_Farokht_Nahi "Ullu Baraye Farokht Nahi")*, *[Shehr-e-Zaat](/wiki/Shehr-e-Zaat "Shehr-e-Zaat")*, *[Suno Chanda](/wiki/Suno_Chanda "Suno Chanda")* and many others started to premiere from July 2020 on [ZEE5](/wiki/ZEE5 "ZEE5") with a premium membership.

[Star India](/wiki/Star_India "Star India") began airing of *[Mera Naam Yousuf Hai](/wiki/Mera_Naam_Yousuf_Hai "Mera Naam Yousuf Hai")* in 2015 on their channel [Star Plus](/wiki/Star_Plus "Star Plus") and thus becoming the channel's first ever Pakistani drama. The series was aired in India, UAE, USA, Ireland, UK, Austria, Europe, Canada and Latin America.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

As per research study of Pakistani popular drama conducted by Indian scholar Jyoti Mehra, North Indian audience gets a sense of cultural familiarity which they can relate to while watching Pakistani drama.[[31]](#cite_note-:2-31) The study also gives credit to the content of Pakistani dramas for its comparative realism with fast pace, shorter duration and limited number of episodes for its acceptability among Indian audience.[[31]](#cite_note-:2-31)

### Other countries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pakistani_dramas&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Other countries")]

Pakistani dramas are shown in [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan"),[[5]](#cite_note-geo.tv-5) [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh"),[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[6]](#cite_note-tribune.com.pk-6) [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal").[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34) Pakistani television shows are aired on [cable television](/wiki/Cable_television "Cable television") channels in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway"), [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey") and [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran") for the [Pakistani diaspora](/wiki/Pakistani_diaspora "Pakistani diaspora").[[35]](#cite_note-35)
